1: Cry out loudly — do not hold back; raise your voice like a trumpet and tell my people their rebellion and the house of Jacob their sins. The shofar (trumpet) was used for covenant assembly, alarm, and proclamation. Using the prophetic voice like a trumpet signals that what follows is a formal covenant-lawsuit indictment (rib). The paradox established immediately: the community being indicted considers itself righteously observant. Calling it my people while indicting them maintains the covenant relationship while naming its violation.
2: Yet day after day they seek me and are eager to know my ways — as though they were a nation that practices righteousness and has not abandoned the law of their God; they ask of me just decrees and are pleased to draw near to God. The devastating description of sincere-seeming religious performance without ethical content. The seeking, eagerness, and pleasure in drawing near are genuine emotional states — this is not cynical hypocrisy but sincere religiosity disconnected from justice. They ask for just decrees while practicing injustice; the disconnection is not noticed from inside the practice.
3: They say, 'Why have we fasted but you do not notice? Why have we denied ourselves and you take no account of it?' Look — on your fast day you pursue your own interests and push all your laborers. The Day of Atonement fast (and other prescribed fast days) required economic suspension: no work, no commerce. But the people are using the fast day to press their laborers for productivity — the denial of self is performed religiously while economic exploitation continues. YHWH notices the fast and the exploitation simultaneously.
4: Look, you fast only to quarrel and brawl and to strike with a wicked fist. You cannot fast as you do today and expect your voice to be heard on high. The social violence accompanying the fasting nullifies the fasting's religious efficacy: the acts of worship are performed alongside the acts of oppression. ANE prayer and sacrifice were understood to be heard by the deity when performed in the correct ritual manner; YHWH declares that no ritual correctness can make audible a prayer accompanied by violence against the vulnerable.
5: Is this the kind of fast I have chosen — merely a day for people to deny themselves, to bow their heads like a reed and spread sackcloth and ashes beneath them? Is that what you call a fast, a day acceptable to the LORD? The reed-bowing posture and sackcloth-and-ashes are the standard ANE and Israelite penitential gestures. YHWH's question is not rhetorical rejection of ritual form but exposure of form without content: the posture of repentance without the reorientation of power toward justice is theater, not worship.
6: Is not this the fast I choose: to release the bonds of injustice, to untie the ropes of the yoke, to set the oppressed free, and to break every yoke? The Jubilee fast — the Day of Atonement of Leviticus 25 announced the Jubilee year of release — is the template here: debt-slavery release, oppression freedom, yoke-breaking. YHWH's chosen fast is an economic and social event, not merely a personal religious exercise. The verbal cluster (release, untie, set free, break) is liberation vocabulary.
7: Is it not to share your food with the hungry, to bring the wandering poor into your home, to clothe the naked when you see them, and not to look away from your own kin? The three acts of practical justice — food-sharing, housing the displaced, clothing the naked — constitute the authentic fast. Not looking away from your own kin is the covenant obligation of family solidarity extended to the community: the poor neighbor is flesh (basar) — literally bodily kin. Averting the eyes from need is itself a covenant violation.
8: Then your light will break out like the dawn and your recovery will come quickly — your righteousness will lead the way before you, and the glory of the LORD will be your rear guard. The light-at-dawn metaphor captures the sudden, unmistakable appearance of covenant blessing after the darkness of injustice. The rear guard is YHWH's glory — the same pillar of cloud and fire that protected Israel at the Exodus (Exodus 14:19-20). The new-Exodus pattern: covenant justice produces the divine presence that led and protected the first Exodus community.
9: Then you will call and YHWH will answer — you will cry and he will say, 'Here I am' (hineni). If you remove the yoke from among you, stop the pointing finger, and stop speaking wickedness. The hineni (here I am) is the divine response to the human call — the covenant call-and-answer restored. The pointing finger (shalach etzba) describes the condescending gesture of accusation or contempt toward the vulnerable: the social habit of marking others as inferior or culpable. Removing it is part of dismantling the oppressive social structure the fast was masking.
10: If you give yourself to the hungry and satisfy the needs of the oppressed — then your light will rise in the darkness and your deepest night will become as bright as noon. The self-giving (tafiq le-ra'ev nafshekha — literally pour out your soul to the hungry) is the extreme form of food-sharing: not giving from surplus but from the self. The darkness-to-noon reversal describes the exile's darkness being illuminated by justice — the ethical act produces the eschatological transformation.
11: The LORD will guide you continually and satisfy you in dry places and strengthen your bones — you will be like a well-watered garden, like a spring whose waters never run dry. The wilderness-provision imagery (guidance in dry places, satisfaction when parched) recapitulates the Exodus experience as template for the restored community's life. The well-watered garden is Eden-language; the spring that never runs dry is the covenant relationship's inexhaustible provision. Dry places that become gardens is the reversal at the heart of all Second Isaiah's restoration promises.
12: Your people will rebuild the ancient ruins — you will raise up the foundations of many generations; you will be called the Repairer of the Breach (Goder Peres), the Restorer of Paths to Live In (Meshovev Netivot). The specific titles — Repairer of the Breach and Restorer of Paths — are covenant-restoration functions. The breach (peres) in a city wall represented the point of maximum vulnerability and the evidence of defeat. Repairing it restored defensibility and honor simultaneously. Restoring paths is infrastructure repair that enables community life. The community that practices justice inherits these constructive roles.
13: If you keep your feet from breaking the Sabbath and refrain from doing as you please on my holy day — if you call the Sabbath a delight and the LORD's holy day worthy of honor; if you honor it by not following your own ways or pursuing your own pleasures or talking idly. The Sabbath oracle closes the chapter by returning to the covenantal marker of identity. The multiple negatives — not breaking, not doing own pleasure, not talking idly — define Sabbath as the structured suspension of self-will. The positive reframing as delight (oneg) is the distinctive Isaianic contribution: Sabbath is not merely obligation but joy.
14: Then you will find your joy in the LORD, and I will make you ride on the heights of the earth — I will nourish you with the heritage of Jacob your father, for the mouth of the LORD has spoken. Riding on the heights of the earth is the dominion imagery of Deuteronomy 32:13 — YHWH making Israel ride the high places of the land in the conquest. The heritage of Jacob is the patriarchal land-promise in its fullest form. The oracle closes with the prophetic authentication formula (ki pi YHWH diber) — the mouth of YHWH has spoken — marking the promise as irrevocable.
